title: Calculating Inference Compute
concept: Inference compute is estimated as: 2 × Parameters FLOPs per generated token, since it only requires the forward pass.

exercises.prompt: Before we calculate FLOPs, what actually is a 'FLOP' in computer science?

options.text: A single basic math calculation (like multiplying numbers)
The physical size of the computer chips

exercises.explain: FLOP stands for Floating-Point Operation, which represents a single basic math calculation (like addition or multiplication) done by a computer.

cards.title: AI Brainpower
cards.body: Ever wondered how much brainpower an AI uses when it replies to your chat prompt? Let's learn the simple math behind calculating inference compute!

cards.title: Inference Compute Formula
cards.body: When an AI generates text (inference), it only performs a forward pass. Because of this, it takes roughly **2 FLOPs (floating-point operations) per parameter** to generate each new token.

cards.title: The Reading Analogy
cards.body: Think of training a model like writing a book (you read and write repeatedly, doing both forward and backward passes). Inference is like reading the book aloud—you only need to read forward, page by page, which takes half the effort per word!

cards.title: Let's Calculate!
cards.body: If you use a 7-billion parameter model (7B) to write a response, it takes:  \n`2 × 7,000,000,000 = 14 Billion FLOPs` of compute to generate just one single token!

cards.title: Inference vs. Training
cards.body: **Myth:** Running a model (inference) takes just as much computing power per token as training it.  \n**Reality:** Inference is much cheaper! It only needs the forward pass, using 2 FLOPs per parameter, while training requires both forward and backward passes (costing 6 FLOPs per parameter).

cards.title: Training vs. Inference Compute
cards.body: Compare how the computation differs when we train a model versus when we run it for inference.

left.text: Requires both forward and backward passes (approx. 6 FLOPs per parameter).

right.text: Requires only the forward pass (approx. 2 FLOPs per parameter).

exercises.prompt: What is the standard formula to estimate the FLOPs needed per generated token during inference?
exercises.explain: We estimate inference compute by multiplying the number of parameters by 2 FLOPs per token generated.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Generating a token (inference) takes less compute per parameter than training on a token.
exercises.explain: Yes! Training requires both forward and backward passes (approx. 6 FLOPs/param), whereas inference only requires the forward pass (approx. 2 FLOPs/param).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe why inference compute is lower than training.
exercises.sentence: Inference compute only requires the [blank] pass to generate a token.
exercises.explain: Inference only requires a forward pass to generate text, which is why it uses 2 FLOPs per parameter.

exercises.prompt: Which AI correctly calculates the inference compute required for an 8 Billion parameter model?

options.text: AI Response A: "An 8 Billion parameter model needs 48 Billion FLOPs per token during inference."
AI Response B: "An 8 Billion parameter model needs 16 Billion FLOPs per token during inference."
AI Response C: "An 8 Billion parameter model needs 8 Billion FLOPs per token during inference."

exercises.explain: AI Response B correctly uses the 2 × Parameters formula (2 × 8B = 16 Billion FLOPs). Option A incorrectly uses the training formula (6 FLOPs per parameter).

exercises.prompt: If a model has 10 billion parameters, how many FLOPs of compute does it take to generate a single token during inference?
exercises.explain: Inference compute per token requires a forward pass of 2 FLOPs per parameter. Therefore, 10 billion parameters × 2 FLOPs/parameter = 20 billion FLOPs (or 20 GFLOPs) per token generated.
